Farn Polrucar- PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ r Fax (916) 322-0886
October 22,2004
C. April Boling, CPA
San Diego County Republican
Central Committee
7185 Navajo Road, Suite L
San Diego, CA92ll9
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-212
Dear Ms. Boling:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the San Diego County
Republican central committee ("SDCRCC') for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').|
QUESTION
How does the SDCRCC report expenditures made by the SDCRCC federal
committee, which supports both state and federal candidates?
CONCLUSION
Your letter raises complex reporting issues that will be addressed by the
Commission emly next year. For this election period only (through December 3l ), the
amount paid by the federal committee should be reported as contributions from the
federal committee members to the state committee with the federal committee acting as
an interrnediary.
I Government Code sections 8 I 000 - 91 0 14. Commission regulations appe ar at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
e+:2


FileNo. A-04-212
Page No. 2
FACTS
Your request concems the proper reporting of expenditures related to a door
hanger paid for and diskibuted by the San Diego County Republican Central Committee.
During our October 12,20M, telephone conversation, you indicated that the SDCRCC is
one entity made up of three bank accounts. One account is established for the purpose of
supporting or opposing federal candidates and is registerd as a federal recipient
committee (the "federal committee')- Two accounts, one set up to receive contributions
under the political party contribution limits for the purpose of supporting or opposing
California state candidates and committees, and another set up to support or oppose other
candidates or issues, me registered as a California state general purpose committee (the
"state committee'). Unless otherrrise spec'ified, you make the decision as to which
account contributions are deposited.
In September, SDCRCC prepared and printed a door hangerurging Republicans
to vote, and providing them with a list of candidates andmeaswes supported by the
committee.
Because the door hanger contained recommendations relating to federal as well as
non-fbderal candidates, you sought guidance from the Federal Election Commission
("FEC') as to the proper reporting of this expenditure.
According to information provided to you by the FEC, fed€ral law requires that
the payme,nt for the door hangers be initially made from federal funds. Since the door
hanger is considered an exempt activity, reimbrnsement for the state portion is a
maximum of 64 percent. You will therefore make apalmrent from the state oommittee to
the federal committee for 64 percent of the cost of the door hangers- The expenditure
will be reportd on Schedule E of Fonn 460 and will be identified as amernber
commrmication (you indicate the member communication provisions apply to this door
hanger).2 You will identifo the zupported candidato or*"a"*"* * r"q.rirud, *d
include subvendor information identifring the underlying vendor.
You seek advice relating to the difference between the 64 percent transfer allowed
by federal law and the actual state benefit. On a space-usod basis, the aurount of the door
hanger relating to state activity is approximately 80 perccnt- This is a 16 percent
difference.
2 "[P]ayments made by a political party for communications to its members who are registered
with that party which would otherwise qualifu as contributions or expeirditures shall be rcportd in
accordance with Article 2 (commencing lvith Section 8/,200) of Chapter 4, and Chapter 4-6 (commencing
with Section 84600), of this title [the Act]." (Section 85312.)


File No. A-04-212
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
Under the Act, a "contribution" is a payment made forpolitical purposes unless
full and adequate consideration is received. (Section 82015(a).)3
During our October lz,z}O4,telephone conversation, you indicated that the
SDCRCC is one entity made up of three bank accounts. One account is established for
the purpose of supporting or opposing fit'eral candidates and is registered as a federal
recipient committee (the "federal committe'). Two ac@unts, one set up to receive
contributions under the political party contribution limits for the purpose of supporting or
opposing California state candidates and committees, and another set up to support or
oppose other candidates or issues, are registered as a California state general purpose
committee (the "state committee'). Unless otherwise specified, you make the decision as
to which account contributions me deposited.
A pa5ment is earmarked for political prrposes wherU at the time of making the
pa5rment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the funds with which the payment
will be commingled will be used to make contributions or experditures. @egulation
l82l50xl).) Factors relevant for determining whether a donorhas reason to know that
his or her payment will be used for political purposes can include the establishd practice
of the organization collecting the payment, and any representations made by the
organizatron when the payment is made. (McCrry Advice Letter, No- A-89-334.) Based
on this, contributors to the SDCRCC have reason to know that some of their money may
be allocated to the state committee. Thereforg whenpayments aremade from the federal
committee that also support or oppose state or local candidates or ballot measures, the
payme,nts result in a contribution to the state committee.
It is anticipated that the Commission will address reporting and related issues
raised by the new federal rules by regulation early next year. For this election period
only (through December 3l), the SDCRCC should report the federal committee's
payments (in this case, the l6 percent difference) toward Califomia state and local
candidates and measures on its state report. The federal mmmittee's share will be
reported on Schedule A as a monetar5r contribution- The contribution will be allocatd on
Schedule A to contributors to the federal committee, based on the amount they have
contributed to the federal committee since January l,z}0/,,with the federal oommittee
acting as an internediary.
The sources and amounts of these conhibutions should be detemrined on a
percentage basis. For examplg assume the total contributions rreived by the federal
committee since January l,200E',is $10O,00O. If the cost ofthe doorhamgers gaid by the
federal committee (the I 6 p€rc€nt) is $ I 0,00O, then I 0 percent of each federal oommitte
donor's payme,nt will be a contribution to the state committee. The slate oommittee must
identifu those persons from whom it receives contributions of $100 or more in a calendar
year- Using this example, the state committee must identi$ each person who contibuted
3 Section 82044 defines the term "payment" to include the rendering of "morey, propertSr, seryices
or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible-"


File No. A-04-212
Page No. 4
at least $ I ,000 to the federal committee since January l, zXK,because I 0 percent of that
person's conkibution equals $100 to the state committee.o In addition, Sch-edule E will
reflect the payment(s) to the applicable vendor(s) for the services. Because Schedule D
should already reflect the total amount ofpaymants made by SDCRCC to support
California candidates and committees, no changes to Schedule D will be necessary.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
G;[ \\t-lr
By 
Trish Mayer
Political Reform Consultant

not addressed in this letter, and will be included as part of the Commissiofs consideration of a proposed
regulation or regulations.
